findings.evidence: time saved, PR velocity, or benchmark
findings.try_today: Open the source: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code

findings.title: Run Bugbot before you push
findings.why: Move review left so the same agent catches bugs on the diff before the PR roundtrip starts.
how_to_steps: Connect the repository to Cursor and enable Bugbot for the repo in the dashboard.
Run /review or /review-bugbot locally before pushing so the agent inspects the exact diff first.
Turn on incremental review or mention-only mode to keep feedback focused on new commits.
Require the Cursor Bugbot check in branch protection once the team trusts the signal.
findings.evidence: Cursor says Bugbot is over 3x faster, 22% cheaper, finds 10% more bugs per review, and 90% of runs now finish in under 3 minutes.
findings.try_today: Enable Bugbot on one active repo and require its check on draft PRs for one week.

findings.title: Index the repo so agents can search by meaning, not just strings
findings.why: Semantic retrieval cuts the time an agent spends hunting for context and reduces follow-up correction loops.
how_to_steps: Open the workspace and let indexing begin automatically; semantic search becomes available at about 80% completion.
Check indexing status or trigger a rebuild from Cursor Settings > Indexing.
Add large generated paths to .cursorignore or .gitignore so the index stays focused on useful code.
Prompt the agent with behaviors like "where do we handle failed payments?" and let it combine semantic search with grep.
findings.evidence: Cursor reports 12.5% higher codebase QA accuracy on average, 0.3% higher code retention overall, 2.6% higher retention on repos with 1,000+ files, and 2.2% more dissatisfied follow-ups when semantic search is unavailable.
findings.try_today: Re-index the main repo, trim noisy paths with .cursorignore, and compare agent answer quality on one thorny codebase question set.

findings.title: Use always-on cloud agents for review, triage, and incident chores
findings.why: Background automations keep review and maintenance work moving without waiting for someone to open the IDE.
how_to_steps: Create a new automation from the Agents Window, cursor.com/automations, or the /automate skill.
Pick a trigger such as pull request opened, CI completed, Slack, webhook, or cron.
Write the prompt and enable only the tools it needs, such as PR comments, Slack, MCP, or memories.
Choose whether it should run against one repo, multiple repos, or no repo, then save and activate it.
findings.evidence: Cursor says Bugbot-like automations run thousands of times a day and have caught millions of bugs; its internal security-review automation has caught multiple vulnerabilities and critical bugs; Runlayer says the setup helps them move faster than teams five times their size.
findings.try_today: Ship one cron or PR-open automation that reviews merged code for missing tests and posts only high-confidence findings.

findings.title: Add Codex CLI as a terminal-first coding agent
findings.why: A local terminal agent gives you long-running code work and PR review loops without being tied to a single editor.
how_to_steps: Install Codex CLI with the Mac or Linux install script, npm, Homebrew, or a release binary.
Run codex and sign in with ChatGPT so it uses the plan you already pay for.
Start code tasks from the terminal and choose an approval mode that matches your risk tolerance.
Escalate longer jobs to Codex cloud or GitHub review flows when you want work to keep running after you close the terminal.
findings.evidence: OpenAI says GPT-5-Codex can work for more than 7 hours on complex tasks, cached cloud tasks cut median completion time by 90%, bottom-decile turns use 93.7% fewer tokens than GPT-5, and Codex reviews the vast majority of internal PRs while catching hundreds of issues per day.
findings.try_today: Install Codex CLI on one workstation and compare one feature branch done with terminal-agent help versus the current manual loop.

findings.title: Treat system-prompt edits like code changes
findings.why: Prompt tweaks can silently degrade code quality, so they need the same gating discipline as production code.
how_to_steps: Run a broad per-model eval suite for every prompt change that affects an agent or coding workflow.
Use ablations to isolate which prompt line changes quality, latency, or token use.
Soak the change before full rollout if it trades off intelligence versus speed or cost.
Roll out gradually and revert immediately if the broader eval set regresses.
findings.evidence: Anthropic reports that one verbosity instruction caused a 3% drop on both Opus 4.6 and 4.7 in a broader evaluation set, and it immediately reverted the change.
findings.try_today: Put your agent prompt under version control and block merges unless the change passes both narrow and broad eval suites.

findings.title: Graduate capability tests into an always-on regression suite
findings.why: Once an agent can reliably do a task, keep that task in the regression bank so quality drift becomes visible immediately.
how_to_steps: Start with 20 to 50 real tasks drawn from bug reports, support tickets, or manual QA checks.
Split the bank into capability evals that are still hard and regression evals that should pass nearly 100% of the time.
Run tasks in a clean environment, use multiple trials where outputs vary, and prefer deterministic graders first.
When a capability task saturates, move it into the continuous regression suite and track latency, token use, and failure rate over time.
findings.evidence: Anthropic recommends capability tasks that later graduate into a continuous regression suite and notes that fixing eval bugs plus scaffold issues moved one CORE-Bench result from 42% to 95%.
findings.try_today: Pick five agent tasks that already feel solved and convert them into a tiny must-pass regression suite.

findings.title: Design containment at the environment layer first
findings.why: Sandbox and egress boundaries keep failures bounded even when the model, a tool, or a user prompt goes off the rails.
how_to_steps: Choose the isolation pattern that matches the user and task: ephemeral container, workspace sandbox, or sealed VM.
Default to network deny and limit writes to the workspace or explicitly mounted paths.
Keep credentials outside the sandbox and pass only scoped session tokens inside.
Delay project-local config parsing until after trust is established, and inspect tool output before it reaches model context.
findings.evidence: Anthropic says its Claude Code sandbox cut permission prompts by 84%, auto mode catches about 83% of overeager behaviors, and Gray Swan prompt-injection success is roughly 0.1% on single attempts.
findings.try_today: Move one internal coding agent into a workspace-only sandbox with network denied by default and audit what access it still truly needs.

findings.title: Put a gateway in front of providers so outages do not stop the app
findings.why: Routing through a gateway gives you model failover, provider order, and request-level visibility instead of a single brittle dependency.
how_to_steps: Route traffic through one gateway endpoint instead of wiring the app directly to a single provider SDK.
Set a primary model and a fallback models list in providerOptions.gateway.
Add provider ordering and fast timeouts so the gateway fails over quickly when a provider stalls.
Inspect modelAttempts and providerAttempts metadata, then monitor latency, spend, and failure patterns by route.
findings.evidence: Vercel says AI Gateway is the same system that has powered v0 for millions of users, sits on infrastructure delivering sub-20ms latency, and removes the single-provider failure mode.
findings.try_today: Wrap one production AI endpoint with a primary model plus two fallbacks and log which model actually serves each request.

findings.title: Make GPT-5 mini the default coding workhorse
findings.why: A cheap high-skill default model lets teams save the expensive model for cases where verification says more horsepower is actually needed.
how_to_steps: Set gpt-5-mini as the default model for routine implementation, repo Q and A, and PR-sized coding tasks.
Keep the loop verification-based so you escalate only when tests fail, tool use gets messy, or the task stalls.
Use lower verbosity for tight edit loops and increase it only when you need fuller plans or explanations.
Track solve rate and spend side by side for mini-default versus full-model escalations.
findings.evidence: OpenAI reports GPT-5 mini scores 71.0% on SWE-bench Verified and 71.6% on Aider polyglot while costing $0.25 per 1M input tokens and $2 per 1M output tokens.
findings.try_today: Change one editor or agent default to GPT-5 mini and keep a simple escalation rule when verification fails.

findings.title: Cache stable repo context instead of resending it every turn
findings.why: Repeated codebase context is expensive and slow; caching it turns long-running coding loops into faster, cheaper interactions.
how_to_steps: Put stable prompt material first, such as system policy, coding standards, tool schema, and repo summary.
Reuse that exact prefix across turns and agent tool loops, appending only the new task delta at the end.
Apply caching first to codebase Q and A, autocomplete, and multi-turn debugging sessions that resend long context.
Measure time to first token and input-token cost before and after the change.
findings.evidence: Anthropic says prompt caching cuts cost by up to 90% and latency by up to 85%; one 100k-token prompt dropped from 11.5s to 2.4s time-to-first-token.
findings.try_today: Cache the stable repo and policy prefix in one high-volume coding assistant and compare TTFT and spend over a day.

findings.title: Cache stable prompt prefixes to cut LLM cost and latency
findings.why: Marking the unchanging part of a prompt (system instructions, tool defs, RAG/codebase context) as cacheable makes repeat calls reuse it at ~10% of input price and skip prefix compute, so cost and time-to-first-token drop sharply.
how_to_steps: Identify the stable prefix (system prompt, tool defs, long context) that repeats across calls and ensure it meets the model minimum (~1,024 tokens).
Add `cache_control: {type: 'ephemeral'}` to those blocks; keep the prefix byte-identical (no injected timestamps, no reordered tools).
Log `cache_read_input_tokens` and `cache_creation_input_tokens` from the response usage to verify hit rates.
For batch/agentic runs, fire calls within the TTL (5-min default or the 1-hour option) and pre-warm the cache before user traffic.
findings.evidence: Anthropic reports up to 90% lower input cost (cache reads at 0.1x) and up to 85% lower time-to-first-token; documented cases cut a bill from $720 -> $72/mo and per-page generation from $0.42 -> $0.06 (86%).
findings.try_today: Add cache_control to the system prompt of one high-volume call and check cache_read_input_tokens on the second request.

findings.title: Swap pip for uv to cut install and CI time
findings.why: uv is a Rust drop-in for pip/venv that resolves and installs dependencies in a fraction of the time, so local setup and every cold-cache CI run get dramatically faster.
how_to_steps: Install uv (`curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh` or `pip install uv`), then confirm with `uv --version`.
In the project, run `uv init` if there is no pyproject.toml, then `uv add -r requirements.txt` to import existing deps and generate uv.lock.
Commit pyproject.toml and uv.lock; recreate environments with `uv sync` and run commands via `uv run <cmd>` instead of activating a venv.
Swap the CI install step to `uv sync --frozen` for deterministic, cache-fast installs, and compare the install stage time before/after.
findings.evidence: Real Python measured ~8x faster than pip (21.4s -> 2.6s installing JupyterLab); a widely cited uncached install dropped 38s -> 3s; one team cut a five-project CI suite from ~9 min to ~4.5 min.
findings.try_today: Run `uv sync` on one repo and switch its CI install step to uv, then compare the install stage timing.

findings.title: Add a merge-queue integrity check so bad merges can't land silently
findings.why: The April 2026 GitHub merge-queue bug silently reverted already-merged work while CI stayed green, because branch protection validates the temp commit, not what actually lands on main. A tree-hash / merge-base check makes that divergence impossible to miss.
how_to_steps: Add a post-merge CI job that recomputes the expected tree for each landed commit and compares it to the tested PR commit's tree (modulo merge-mode normalization); fail loudly on mismatch.
Verify the merge group's base equals the current head of the target branch before the merge finalizes, and block if they diverge.
Until you trust the queue, cap merge-group size to 1 (sequential) for squash groups to avoid the multi-PR failure combination.
Keep enough audit trail (tested commit SHAs) that you can reconstruct what CI actually signed off on after the fact.
findings.evidence: The April 23, 2026 GitHub merge-queue bug silently corrupted 658 repositories and 2,092 PRs for ~4.5 hours with green CI and no status-page entry; recovery had to be done by hand.
findings.try_today: Add a post-merge job that compares the landed tree hash to the tested PR's tree and alerts on mismatch.

findings.title: Run mutation testing to find tests that don't actually assert
findings.why: High line coverage can hide tests that execute code without verifying behavior. Mutation testing injects small bugs and checks your suite catches them, exposing real gaps a coverage number will not.
how_to_steps: Pick the mutation runner for your stack (Stryker for JS/TS/.NET, PIT for Java, mutmut for Python, cargo-mutants for Rust).
Scope the first run to one core module so it finishes fast, since mutation testing is CPU-heavy.
Review surviving mutants and, for each, add or strengthen an assertion until it is killed instead of chasing the score.
Wire it into CI nightly (or on changed files only) against critical domain logic, not every PR.
findings.evidence: Teams with 95-98% line coverage found ~23% of mutants survived and uncovered 12 real undetected bugs; another 98%-coverage suite still let a data-corrupting bug reach production because tests never asserted the outcome.
findings.try_today: Run the mutation runner on one core module and fix the first three surviving mutants with real assertions.

findings.title: Turn on Turborepo remote caching in CI
findings.why: Remote caching shares task outputs across teammates and CI runners, so unchanged builds/tests/lints download instead of re-running, collapsing duplicated work on every PR.
how_to_steps: Define cacheable tasks in turbo.json with precise `inputs` and `outputs` so content hashes are correct.
Connect a remote cache: managed via `npx turbo login` + `npx turbo link`, or a self-hosted cache endpoint with a token.
Run a build twice locally and confirm the second run reports a remote cache hit.
In CI, pass the cache token/team env vars and run `turbo run build test lint`; verify the logs show 'remote cache hit'.
findings.evidence: Makeswift cut CI pipeline time 65% (commit-to-deploy ~20 min -> under 1 min); the Next.js team cut publish times 80%; a 6-month monorepo retrospective reported full builds down ~50% (22 -> 11 min p50) at a 94% cache hit rate.
findings.try_today: Enable remote caching on one repo and re-run a green build to confirm a remote cache hit.

findings.title: Give every PR an ephemeral full-stack preview environment
findings.why: CI is structurally blind to deployed-state, real-data-shape, third-party-drift, browser, and cross-service bugs. A per-PR deployed environment surfaces those before merge instead of in production.
how_to_steps: Describe the full stack (app + backend services + DB + queues/caches) as code with Compose, Helm, or Terraform.
Trigger provisioning on PR open via CI, giving each PR an isolated namespace/stack and a unique HTTPS URL.
Seed or clone an isolated database per environment so branches never share state.
Auto-destroy the environment on merge/close and post the preview URL to the PR for reviewers and QA.
findings.evidence: Per-PR preview environments target five bug classes CI cannot catch (deployed-state, real-data-shape, third-party integration drift, browser regressions, cross-component flows); teams report catching UI/integration bugs before merge and removing shared-staging contention.
findings.try_today: Stand up a per-PR preview for one service and share the URL on your next PR for QA.
